text: Today we are talking about gastric surgery and exactly we are not talking about a routine intervention but about the problems and complications that can arise during a total gastrectomy intervention.

The surgeon dissects the area after opening the gastrocolic ligament and the epiplomb's electrocavity, carefully preserving the Frédé band to release station number six of the right gastroepiploic vessels.

The surgeon performs dissection by smoothing, with the gastrocolic ligament opened extensively. In this corpulent patient, the anatomical planes are more easily recognized, aided by the insufflation of gas during the procedure.
The surgeon performs very gentle maneuvers to avoid cutting the transverse mesocolon during the dissection process.
The surgeon isolates the right gastrointestinal vein, carefully preserving the Henle trunk visible immediately below it.
The surgeon applies clips to ligate the gastro-bibloid vessels and proceeds to section them, aiming to dissect these vessels in a single block.
The surgeon dissects the gastro-bibloid artery, revealing it clearly. During the dissection of the periapital space, a small leakage is observed, which is controlled using a bipolar clamp.
The surgeon attempts to coagulate the orifice of the artery, but the attempt is unsuccessful. An erroneous attempt is made to blindly clip the artery, which is visually evident.
The surgeon uses bipolar coagulation or radiofrequency to dissect the tissue, followed by sectioning with an ultrasound device.
During the dissection, a hemorrhage occurs, obscuring the camera's view. The surgical team cleans the camera and re-enters the field to locate and control the source of bleeding.
The surgeon identifies the bleeding source beneath the clamp and uses suction and washing to reduce the bleeding. With the assistance of the left hand, the bleeding hole is located and closed.

The surgical field is cleaned using aspiration, allowing for better visualization and identification of a potential bleeding source. A specific area is highlighted where the bleeding is more easily identifiable.

The surgeon approaches the bleeding site and uses washing to identify the hole in the gastrointestinal artery on the right. A clip is placed on the artery, but bleeding persists, prompting the placement of a second clip nearby. The clamp is gently adjusted to identify the highest position, allowing the continuation of the surgical intervention with the sectioning of the gastroepiploic artery near the duodenal gastro, followed by the dissection of the duodenum.
The surgeon places gauze for hemostatic control and to mark anatomical landmarks, then identifies the lilyopathic structure within the surgical field.

The surgeon identifies a lymphagitic condition in the lilyopathic area, possibly neoplastic or fibrotic, using traction and counter-traction maneuvers to expose the fat cells near the liver and main biliary structures.
The surgeon releases the common hepatic artery, placing it on a colored vessel loop for identification and mobilization. The main biliary duct is visible laterally to the artery.

The surgeon identifies and releases the cystic duct near its origin, leaving the cystic cord en bloc in the operative field. The anatomy becomes clearer, and a duodenal section is performed to fully separate the operative area from the working field.
The surgeon identifies the right hepatic artery, which courses laterally to the common hepatic duct. Station XIII, located laterally to the common hepatic duct, is isolated and dissected.
The surgeon identifies the biliary tract, which is visually adherent to the right hepatic artery, showing tenacious adhesion sequences between these structures.

The surgeon performs gentle dissection maneuvers to remove a cell closely connected to the common hepatic duct. The common hepatic duct and the right and left hepatic ducts are identified, and the main bile duct is underpassed with a vessel loop. The anatomy is highlighted, showing the common hepatic duct, right and left branches, and the right hepatic artery running behind the common hepatic duct. Below, the portal branch is visible, and station XIII emerges as the main duct is dissected to the left along the Smussa road.

The surgeon isolates station number thirteen using ultrasonic dissection, carefully sectioning the tip of the station. During this process, it becomes apparent that station number thirteen is being drawn towards itself, revealing an aberrant right hepatic accessory emerging from the choledochus.

The surgeon identifies a modest leakage and attempts to suture it using two Prolene 6-0 sutures, checking the area multiple times to ensure reduction of the leakage before continuing with the surgical intervention.

The surgeon closes the aberrant duct at the level of the sinus in the common bile duct and removes it for histological examination. The leakage appears well defined, and the right branch is highlighted, showing its normal course.

The surgeon performs a regional esophagus anastomosis, isolating the esophagus by seven to eight centimeters under the guidance of a gastrointestinal tube. The anastomosis is secured with a mucous suture on the esophagus to ensure proper holding.

The surgeon positions a linear stapler on the right side of the esophagus, using a 45mm staple load. The esophagus is partially transected, revealing a complication where the probe becomes trapped, pulling the anastomotic block towards the mediastinum. Efforts are made to locate the probe both palpatorily and visually, as it is not easily detectable, leading to an evaluation of the upper margin where the probe may be trapped.

The surgeon completes the suture and the anesthesiologist performs a forced operation. The probe is removed, revealing two clips from the mechanical suturing that secured the probe. The probe is repositioned to complete the nostromatic eye on its guide.

The surgeon uses a 3-0 monofilament suture to perform continuous sutures on the esophagus and jejunal esophagus. The right margin is sutured with a seromuscular technique, consolidating the previous suture line and securing the esophagus in the intrathoracic position. The remaining esophagus is excised, and a second suture line is completed on the left side, ensuring the seromuscular layer is incorporated and the suture line is closed.

This double suture performed on the larynx is a suture that at first glance may not seem very good, but now with the consolidated experience of more than ten years of the execution of these anastomosis, let's say that if performed on a guide by means of a probe, it has no problems.

At this point we define the second step, which is to go and see what was the situation at the level of the road. Here we notice that there is a persistent leakage, also important, Consequently, given that the intervention had various complications, and above all, it was protracted for a period of time of five hours, it is not so much because, as Maruyama said, to perform a good and correct DII, necessarily the surgical time should not be less than four and a half hours.

However, in order not to create short circuits and in order to perform a better quality of anastomosis, it is preferred to perform a conversion by means of a right subcostal incision and perform a hepatic-digital derivation on ANSA, also excluded from RU, so the final result is that of the highlighted graph, and the patient has two trans-mesocolic diurnal anuses. I think this was a very correct thing from my point of view, to perform a conversion only for esophageal diurnal anastomosis, because only with the help of robotic arms we could perform an anastomosis so small, because this remains an aberrant bile duct or accessory of the right.

At this point the intervention is finished and as it is normally performed with an ERAS also in the upper GI, a radiological control of the esophageal-digital anastomosis is performed in the first post-operative day and the patient begins a feeding in the first hydric day and in the second day free of bones. In the first post-operative day, the presence of about four hundred cc of bile material per day remains in the bile drainage under the bile anastomosis, which is controlled in the following days. This remains for about when we decide to perform a drainage fistulography where we can see that there is a good anastomosis because the medium of contrast passes partly in the biliar duct and we recognize it as a biliar duct accessory on the right also for the size and for the shortness, with the contrast medium that brilliantly goes inside the anus. At this point we see that it is the drainage to maintain this loss, so under radiological guidance the drainage is gradually mobilized, which was probably behaving almost like a care drainage, and at this point the progressive removal leads to a reduction until the total disappearance of the drainage biliary material and the patient is sent home on the twenty-fifth post-operative day in excellent general conditions.

This is to say that during a surgical intervention of neoplastic pathology, therefore after a lymphadenatomy, complications may occur, which are not real complications, but rather a problem that can be evidenced during a complex surgical intervention. The important thing is to know how to deal with them and to be able to correct a possible alteration.
